summary:
The Carnation Revolution of April 25, 1974 was a near-bloodless military coup in Portugal that ended forty-eight years of authoritarian rule under the Estado Novo regime and rapidly led to the transition to democracy. The coup was carried out by the Armed Forces Movement (MFA), a group of junior and mid-ranking military officers who had been radicalized by the futile [[event:3467|colonial wars]] in Africa. Within hours, the old regime had collapsed with minimal resistance, and jubilant Lisbon residents placed red carnations in the muzzles of the soldiers' rifles, giving the revolution its name.

The transition that followed was turbulent, involving competing factions of the military, left-wing political parties, and the Communist Party, with a period of near-revolutionary upheaval in 1975. However, Portugal emerged from this transition with a functioning parliamentary democracy. The Carnation Revolution also rapidly ended Portuguese colonialism: Angola, Mozambique, Guinea-Bissau, Cape Verde, and São Tomé and Príncipe all achieved independence in 1974–1975. Portugal subsequently joined the European Community in 1986, completing its transformation from authoritarian colonial power to democratic European state.
